title: Convergence — where a revolution comes from
subtitle: Start at the transformer. Trace its roots back across four fields.

steps.title: A revolution, fully formed
steps.body: "Attention Is All You Need" — the architecture behind today's large language models. We've parked on it, its details open, surrounded by the work it set in motion. From here it looks like it sprang fully formed out of computer science. It didn't. Every revolution has roots — so let's trace where this one actually came from, backward across forty years and several fields.

steps.title: The sky resolves into fields
steps.body: Now switch on the field map: every paper grouped by its discipline, each field its own glowing galaxy. The distant fields fold away into clouds — we can't see inside them from here. But notice the faint threads still reaching out toward them: connections leave Computer Science and run into other disciplines entirely. The ideas this architecture was built from are out there, upstream. Let's follow a thread back.
steps.nextLabel: Reach back, out of computer science →

steps.title: Reach back, out of computer science
steps.body: Follow one of those threads. Ask the graph for a route from the transformer back toward neuroscience — and it draws one: out of Computer Science, through a single 2020 review that looked both forward and back, and into a 1982 paper in another field entirely. Just two hops, every link a real citation. The path lights up, threading through the folded fields it crosses — the rest of them still dark.

steps.title: The ancestor: a 1982 idea in neuroscience
steps.body: Fly the course. We leave computer science, pass through that lone bridge, and arrive — in neuroscience, on a paper from 1982. A physicist's model of memory: a network of simple units settling into the stable states of an energy landscape. Four decades before the transformer, in a different field entirely, here is an ancestor of the idea.

steps.title: Older still — and from physics
steps.body: The energy landscape wasn't born in neuroscience either. Reach one more step back, and a single citation leaves Hopfield's paper and crosses into another galaxy entirely — Physics. It lands on a 1986 work on spin glasses: disordered magnets whose mathematics of settling into low-energy states is exactly what Hopfield borrowed to make a network remember. Plot the course out of neuroscience and into physics.

steps.title: Into physics
steps.body: Make the crossing. We leave neuroscience behind and land in physics — on a 1986 study of spin glasses, disordered magnets. It's the same mathematics: a rugged energy landscape, a system relaxing into a stable low-energy state. Physicists reached for it to explain magnets; Hopfield reached back for it to make a network remember. The transformer's lineage now runs through three fields and forty years — and the trail behind us stays lit.

steps.title: Back to the meeting point
steps.body: Before the last root, come back to where the lines cross — Hopfield's 1982 paper. Look at the trail we've drawn: the transformer's lineage runs back to here, and from here a thread already reaches out into physics. This is the convergence point — the place a single idea, the energy landscape, was borrowed into a model of memory. There's one more field it came from.

steps.title: Older still — and from biology
steps.body: The last root reaches the other way — out of neuroscience and into biology. A single citation leaves Hopfield's paper for a 1997 study of protein folding, where a chain of amino acids slides down its own energy landscape and settles into a stable shape. The same idea once more — magnets, memories, molecules — three distant fields, one piece of mathematics. Plot the course into biochemistry.

steps.title: Into biology
steps.body: Make the final crossing. We leave Hopfield once more and land in biochemistry, on the 1997 protein-folding paper. Three distant fields have now handed the same idea forward — disordered magnets, a model of memory, a folding molecule — each a rugged energy landscape, each a system settling into its lowest state.

steps.title: Convergence
steps.body: Pull back and look at the whole trail. One architecture — the transformer — and a lineage that runs back across five fields and forty years: computer science, engineering, neuroscience, physics, biology. None of them set out to build artificial intelligence. A revolution rarely arrives from nowhere; it's distant ideas, from fields that never meet, converging on a single point.
